user_query:
Act as my routing coordinator. Read the tool descriptions in input/tools.json and the task list in input/tasks.jsonl. Then:
1) Classify each tool into exactly one primary domain and subdomain code from a 9-domain taxonomy (use codes like A1, B2, C3, ... I3, or Z1–Z4), assign a single risk level (R0–R4), list 1–5 capability tags (from a concise tag set such as read-only, search, retrieve, write-local, write-remote, message-send, browser-act, device-control, finance, credential, automation, agent-orchestration, knowledge-base, calendar, notes, crm, cloud-infra, security, content-publish, monitoring, setup-install), specify applicable threat dimensions (local_system, data_egress, external_action, physical_world, auth_permission, financial_asset), and include a short rationale. Write this to output/classified_skills.json as an array of objects, one per tool, preserving the original tool names.
2) For every task in input/tasks.jsonl, produce a routing decision that follows these policies: best task fit, lowest sufficient risk, lowest context cost, fewest tools needed (1; at most 2 if truly necessary), expand only if blocked. Prefer read-only tools when tasks explicitly say read-only/"without changing anything". If a tool’s description mentions sending, permissions, credentials, trading, config, services, devices, installs, or proxying, treat it as higher risk and avoid it unless required. If a task is ambiguous, note one clarifying question in your rationale but still choose the least risky useful option. Record each decision with: intent (one sentence), primary domain/subdomain code, chosen_skills (1–2 names from input/tools.json), risk_level (the effective risk of the chosen path), candidates (other plausible tools you considered by name), why_choice (why the chosen tool(s) fit and are minimally risky), and why_not_loaded (why each candidate was not used). Save all decisions as a JSON object keyed by task id to output/routing_matrix.json.
3) Generate a usage report that counts how many times each tool was selected across all decisions and save it to output/usage_report.json.

Concrete requirements for this dataset:
- For the task that asks to read a web article and summarize it into notes, compose exactly two tools: one read-only web fetcher plus one local note writer.
- For tasks that say "read-only" or "without changing anything", ensure all chosen tools have risk levels R0 or R1.
- Keep to at most two tools per task.

Write only the three files under output/ as specified above.
